name: Sangreal Bloodline
description: The Sangreal Bloodline theory posits that the Holy Grail, often envisioned as a cup, is actually a metaphor for a bloodline descended from Jesus Christ and Mary Magdalene. Proponents claim that this bloodline continued through the Merovingian dynasty of Frankish kings and persists to this day in various European families. The theory gained prominence through books like 'Holy Blood, Holy Grail' which popularized the idea. Evidence cited includes interpretations of religious texts, genealogical research suggesting Merovingian descent in certain families, and symbolic interpretations of art and architecture. Mainstream historians and theologians largely dismiss the Sangreal Bloodline theory as lacking credible evidence. They criticize the selective use of historical sources, misinterpretations of religious symbolism, and reliance on pseudoscientific genealogical methods. The historical record provides no conclusive proof of a marriage between Jesus and Mary Magdalene or any surviving descendants. The theory is often associated with anti-establishment sentiments and revisionist history, challenging traditional Christian narratives and power structures. The romantic appeal lies in the idea of a hidden truth and a royal lineage with divine origins, offering an alternative narrative to orthodox religious history. The theory has been widely popularized in fiction, further blurring the lines between historical speculation and imaginative storytelling.
subtitle: Descendants of Jesus and Mary Magdalene?

claims.claim: Jesus Christ and Mary Magdalene were married and had children.
claims.evidence: Apocryphal gospels, interpretations of artistic symbolism.
claims.criticism: No historical evidence to support marriage; apocryphal texts are not considered canonical.

claims.claim: The Merovingian dynasty was descended from Jesus and Mary Magdalene.
claims.evidence: Genealogical claims linking Merovingians to the House of David, interpretations of Merovingian symbolism.
claims.criticism: Genealogical claims are tenuous and lack solid historical basis; symbolic interpretations are subjective.

claims.claim: The Sangreal bloodline continues to exist in modern European families.
claims.evidence: Genealogical research, secret societies preserving the lineage.
claims.criticism: Genealogical claims are often based on circumstantial evidence and conjecture; existence of secret societies is not definitively proven.

keyFigures.name: Dan Brown
keyFigures.role: Popularized the theory in 'The Da Vinci Code'

keyFigures.name: Marina Warner
keyFigures.role: Critic of the theory; historian and mythographer

timeline.event: Emergence of Grail legends in Arthurian romances
